expected_outcome: IntelliDeceptor will offer a more dynamic and responsive honeypot system capable of handling complex and varied cyber threats.

topic: agents swarm
title: SwarmForge: A Lightweight Tool Library for Reproducible Agent Swarm Experiments with Automated Metric Collection and Error Reporting

provenance.resources_text: Development will require a small team (2-3 developers) with expertise in Python, software engineering best practices, and agent-based modeling. Existing open-source libraries for agent simulation (e.g., Mesa, RLlib) can be leveraged. The project will be hosted on GitHub with CI/CD pipelines to ensure compatibility. Documentation and example notebooks will be created to facilitate adoption.

motivation: Reproducing agent swarm experiments is challenging due to inconsistent environment setups, lack of standard performance metrics, and opaque error handling. Many published studies do not provide sufficient detail to rerun experiments, and when they do, researchers often struggle with missing dependencies, undocumented parameters, and ambiguous evaluation protocols. A lightweight, extensible tool library that standardizes the process of configuring, executing, and evaluating agent swarm simulations would significantly lower the barrier to reproducibility. This library would automate the collection of performance metrics and capture detailed error logs, ensuring that experiments can be rerun successfully and results can be compared across studies.
hypothesis: By providing a standardized, easy-to-use tool library for agent swarm experiments, researchers will be able to reproduce existing studies with higher success rates and produce more reliable, comparable performance metrics. Additionally, the automated error reporting will reduce the time spent diagnosing failures and improve the transparency of experimental results.
method_sketch: SwarmForge will be implemented as a Python package that integrates with existing agent frameworks (e.g., OpenAI Gym, PettingZoo, or custom environments). It will provide: 1) A configuration manager that validates experiment parameters and ensures consistent seeding. 2) A runner that executes the swarm simulation, captures stdout/stderr, and logs all events. 3) A metrics collector that computes standard swarm performance indicators (e.g., convergence time, success rate, communication overhead) and allows custom metric definitions. 4) An error reporting module that records exceptions, environment state, and agent states for debugging. The library will be designed to be framework-agnostic, requiring minimal changes to existing code. It will also include a command-line interface to rerun experiments from a configuration file and generate a reproducibility report.
expected_outcome: A fully functional Python library (SwarmForge) released under an open-source license. The library will be tested by reproducing at least three prominent agent swarm studies from the literature, demonstrating improved reproducibility and metric consistency. We will publish a paper describing the tool, its design, and the reproduction results. Adoption by the community will be encouraged through workshops and tutorials.
metrics: convergence time, success rate, communication overhead

motivation: Reproducing agent swarm experiments is challenging due to heterogeneous frameworks, inconsistent performance metrics, and lack of error logging standards. The library will be designed as a lightweight wrapper, allowing researchers to plug in their own agent implementations.
expected_outcome: A publicly available Python library (SwarmBench) with documentation and examples. The library will be evaluated by reproducing at least three diverse agent swarm experiments from recent papers, demonstrating successful execution, metric collection, and error reporting. The evaluation will show reduced setup time and increased reproducibility.

title: Machine learning-driven optimization of crystalline admixture dosage and curing conditions for maximum durability against sulfate attack

provenance.resources_text: Access to existing literature databases; experimental facilities for generating additional data points; computational resources for ML; software (Python with scikit-learn, TensorFlow, etc.).

motivation: The performance of crystalline admixtures (CAs) is highly dependent on dosage, cement composition, and curing conditions. Traditional trial-and-error approaches are time-consuming and costly. Leveraging machine learning (ML) can accelerate the discovery of optimal mixes and curing regimes to maximize resistance to sulfate attack. This approach integrates experimental data with predictive modeling to provide a robust framework for mix design.
hypothesis: Machine learning can accurately predict the sulfate resistance of CA-modified cementitious materials, and the optimization algorithm will yield mixes with significantly improved durability compared to conventional formulations.
method_sketch: We will compile a comprehensive dataset from literature and our own experiments on CA performance under sulfate attack, including variables: cement type, CA type, dosage, water-cement ratio, curing time, curing temperature, sulfate concentration, and exposure duration. Response variables: expansion strain, compressive strength loss, pore size distribution parameters (e.g., critical pore diameter, total porosity). We will train ML models (e.g., random forest, gradient boosting, neural networks) to predict these responses. Feature importance analysis will identify dominant factors. Then, using genetic algorithms, we will optimize the input variables to minimize expansion while satisfying cost constraints. The optimized mixes will be experimentally validated.
expected_outcome: 1) A curated dataset of CA performance under sulfate attack. 2) ML models with high predictive accuracy (R2 >0.85). 3) Identification of optimal CA dosages and curing conditions for various cement types. 4) Experimental confirmation that optimized mixes reduce expansion by at least 40% compared to standard recommendations.
dataset: A curated dataset of CA performance under sulfate attack

title: A Global Database of Social-Ecological Water Management Cases: Integration of Socio-Economic, Environmental, and Institutional Indicators for Comparative Analysis and Machine Learning

provenance.resources_text: Computing resources for data scraping, NLP, and database hosting; access to academic databases (Scopus, Web of Science); collaboration with international water organizations for data sharing; funding for research assistants to curate data; use of open-source tools (Python, PostgreSQL, Docker).

motivation: The frontier paper highlights the need for SES as a unit of analysis for sustainable water management under anthropogenic pressure. However, current databases are fragmented, often limited to specific regions or disciplinary perspectives, lacking integrated socio-ecological indicators. A comprehensive, globally curated dataset linking water system attributes, ecological stress, governance structures, and community outcomes would enable robust comparative research, meta-analyses, and predictive modeling to inform policy and practice.
hypothesis: A harmonised global database of social-ecological water systems will reveal key patterns and leverage points for sustainable water management, and enable the development of predictive models of system resilience under various anthropogenic pressures.
method_sketch: We will compile a global dataset from existing databases (e.g., UN Water, FAO AQUASTAT, WRI Aqueduct, OECD Water Governance, World Bank) and primary case studies from literature (systematic review). For each case (river basin, aquifer, coastal zone, etc.), we will extract standardised variables: physical (climate, hydrology, water quality, biodiversity), socio-economic (population, water use, economic sectors), institutional (governance type, regulations, participation), and sustainability outcomes (water security index, ecological status, community well-being). Natural language processing (NLP) will be used to extract data from grey literature and reports. The dataset will be structured in a relational database with API access. We will also develop a classification schema for SES archetypes using clustering techniques.
expected_outcome: A publicly available dataset with at least 10,000 cases, documented metadata, and a web portal for exploration. Publications on dataset description, archetype analysis, and predictive modeling of water sustainability.
dataset: UN Water, FAO AQUASTAT, WRI Aqueduct, OECD Water Governance, World Bank

title: Theoretical Framework for Integrating Multimodal Creative Agents into Multi-Agent Reasoning Systems

provenance.resources_text: No datasets or compute heavy resources needed; only access to literature (papers from both clusters) and possibly lightweight simulation environments (e.g., Python prototypes for proof-of-concept). Software: modeling tools (UML, Alloy) for formal specification.

motivation: Autonomous agent research has evolved along two parallel streams: one focusing on reasoning, planning, and coordination (Cluster A) and another on multimodal creative generation (Cluster B). While both aim to advance AI autonomy, they remain disconnected. Bridging these streams could enable more sophisticated AI systems that combine high-level reasoning with multimodal creative execution, such as autonomous research agents that not only plan experiments but also generate explanatory animations or videos. This integration is currently missing, and a theoretical analysis can lay the groundwork for future empirical systems.
hypothesis: A well-defined theoretical framework can seamlessly integrate reasoning-focused multi-agent systems with multimodal creative agents, enabling new classes of autonomous systems without requiring significant modifications to existing agent architectures.
method_sketch: We will develop a formal theoretical model that maps the components of reasoning/planning agents (e.g., from Agentic RAG, DOVA) to the requirements of multimodal creative agents (e.g., Anim-Director, MSVBench). The model will define interfaces, communication protocols, and knowledge representation schemes that allow a multi-agent reasoning system to delegate creative tasks to multimodal agents. The analysis will involve: (1) taxonomy of agent capabilities from both clusters; (2) identification of compatibility gaps; (3) proposition of a unified architecture with theoretical guarantees on information flow and consistency; (4) simulation of abstract scenarios to validate feasibility.
expected_outcome: A comprehensive theoretical model published as a conference paper, including formal specifications, interoperability guidelines, and a set of open research challenges. The model will be validated through illustrative case studies (e.g., a research automation agent that produces an animated summary of its findings).

topic: Autonomous Novelty Agents in Real-Time Chat
title: Adaptive Ethical Framework for Dynamic Chatbot Content Generation

provenance.resources_text: To develop AEF, we require: 1) a diverse, ethically annotated conversational dataset for training and validation; 2) computational resources for neural network training (e.g., 4-8 GPUs); 3) Python software stack (PyTorch/TensorFlow) for model development; 4) an environment for deploying and testing the chatbot in real-time scenarios.

motivation: The rapid integration of AI-powered chatbots into various sectors necessitates a robust framework for ethically aligned content generation. Existing systems lack dynamic protocols to prevent the generation of inappropriate content, posing significant ethical and societal risks. This research aims to develop an Adaptive Ethical Framework (AEF) that can adapt to nuanced ethical dilemmas in real-time, ensuring ethical content generation while maintaining creativity.
hypothesis: AEF will reduce the generation of inappropriate content by at least 30% in real-time chat scenarios, as measured by a newly proposed Content Appropriateness Score (CAS), which combines elements of BLEU score with ethical compliance ratings.
method_sketch: AEF is a system design that incorporates a dynamic ethical protocol within a novelty-generating chatbot. The system includes: 1) a contextual sentiment analysis module using pre-trained embeddings to capture conversational nuances; 2) a neural network-based content screening mechanism that aligns with a set of predefined ethical parameters; 3) a novel ethical parameter learning module that adapts the ethical guidelines in real-time based on user feedback and context-specific ethical dilemmas; 4) a reinforcement learning module that allows for real-time adaptation and refinement of ethical guidelines based on predefined ethical criteria. The system will be trained and validated on a diverse, ethically annotated conversational dataset.
expected_outcome: The successful implementation of AEF will result in a demonstrable reduction in harmful outputs from novelty agents in chats, validated through the proposed Content Appropriateness Score (CAS). This score will provide a quantitative measure of the system's performance in balancing creativity with ethical content generation.

topic: CHAT WITH NEW NOVELTY IDEAS WITH AGENTS
title: Learned Generator Sets for Task-Optimal Cayley Communication Graphs

provenance.resources_text: Simulation environment for multi-agent tasks (e.g., consensus, flocking) with up to 500 agents; deep reinforcement learning library (PyTorch, RLlib); Cayley graph generation code.

motivation: CayleyTopo optimizes for diameter alone, but in many multi-agent tasks (e.g., consensus, formation control, distributed optimization), the communication topology should be optimized for task-specific objectives like convergence rate or robustness to noise. This idea combines algebraic graph theory with reinforcement learning to learn generator sets that are tailored to the task.
hypothesis: Learned Cayley topologies will achieve up to 20% faster convergence and 15% better noise robustness compared to diameter-optimal Cayley graphs and other sparse topologies like random regular graphs, on a range of multi-agent coordination tasks.
method_sketch: Define a parameterized family of Cayley graphs on the cyclic group Z_n, where the generator set is represented as a binary mask vector. Use a policy gradient method (e.g., REINFORCE) to learn the mask that maximizes a task-specific reward (e.g., inverse of consensus time, or robustness measured by algebraic connectivity). The policy network takes as input task descriptors (e.g., number of agents, noise level) and outputs a probability distribution over generator masks. Training is done in a simulated environment with differentiable graph operators (e.g., using PyTorch Geometric). The learned generator sets are then deployed as a library of topologies for different task conditions.
expected_outcome: A new method for task-adaptive communication topology design, demonstrated on multiple tasks, and an open-source library of pre-trained generator sets for common task classes.
metrics: convergence rate, robustness measured by algebraic connectivity
baselines: diameter-optimal Cayley graphs, random regular graphs

topic: computational social science agent based modeling and simulation
title: A Theoretical Framework for Epistemic Validation of Generative Agent-Based Social Simulations

provenance.resources_text: No datasets or compute needed for theoretical analysis; access to literature (papers from both clusters, plus philosophy of simulation, validation methods). Software for conceptual modeling (e.g., Miro) and writing.

motivation: The integration of LLMs into agent-based models (ABMs) is rapidly advancing computational social science, but it raises profound epistemological questions: How do we validate simulations that generate synthetic human behavior? Traditional ABM validation relies on empirical data and theoretical grounding; LLM-driven agents introduce opaque, data-driven behavior that may not reflect stable human cognition. This research bridges the need for temporal stability and consistency (Cluster A) with the evolutionary shift in computational social science from observational to generative data (Cluster B). By developing a theoretical framework for epistemic validation—assessing not just predictive accuracy but also explanatory power, interpretability, and alignment with social theory—we can guide the design of LLM-ABM integrations, ensuring they contribute meaningfully to social science knowledge rather than being black-box curiosities.
hypothesis: A well-defined epistemic validation framework will reveal gaps in current LLM-ABM practices and provide a pathway for more rigorous, theoretically grounded simulations that can be trusted for policy and social science insights.
method_sketch: We will conduct a theoretical analysis that synthesizes concepts from philosophy of science (epistemic virtues), ABM validation literature, and recent work on LLM-based simulation. The framework will propose a set of criteria and methods for evaluating generative ABMs: (1) Temporal stability & consistency metrics; (2) Causal alignment (whether agent behaviors can be mapped to known psychological/social mechanisms); (3) Interpretability of emergent phenomena; (4) Ethical robustness (bias detection and mitigation). We will illustrate the framework by critically examining existing LLM-ABM studies (e.g., from Cluster A) and proposing a roadmap for future empirical validation standards. The output will be a structured taxonomy and a checklist for researchers.
expected_outcome: A comprehensive theoretical paper defining epistemic validation criteria for LLM-ABMs, with a proposed validation protocol. The paper will be submitted to a top-tier computational social science journal (e.g., JASSS) and could become a reference for the field.

topic: computational social science agent based modeling and simulation
title: A Theoretical Framework for Evaluating Temporal Stability and Behavioral Realism of LLM Personas in Agent-Based Simulations

provenance.resources_text: No primary data collection needed; relies on existing literature, conceptual analysis. Requires access to academic databases for literature review. No specific compute or software beyond standard research tools.

motivation: Agent-based modeling (ABM) increasingly incorporates LLM-generated personas to simulate complex human behavior. However, two critical open questions from the clusters remain: (1) ensuring temporal stability of LLM personas over long simulations (Cluster A) and (2) comparing the behavioral realism of LLM personas to human participants (Cluster B). A unified theoretical analysis bridging these clusters is needed to provide a rigorous foundation for evaluating and validating LLM-based ABMs. This framework would help researchers design more reliable simulations, address ethical concerns, and establish trust in LLM-generated agent behavior.
hypothesis: A unified theoretical framework that explicitly links temporal stability and behavioral realism will enable more systematic evaluation of LLM personas in ABM, leading to improved simulation validity and reduced risk of bias and inconsistency.
method_sketch: Develop a theoretical framework that integrates concepts from dynamical systems, computational linguistics, and validation theory. The framework will define metrics for temporal stability (e.g., consistency of persona traits over time, drift detection) and behavioral realism (e.g., alignment with human reference data, predictive accuracy). It will also propose methods for calibrating LLM personas using hybrid approaches (e.g., combining rule-based constraints with generative flexibility) and outline validation protocols that combine both stability and realism assessments. The analysis will be grounded in existing literature from both clusters, synthesizing insights into a cohesive set of principles and best practices.
expected_outcome: A peer-reviewed journal article presenting the theoretical framework, including definitions, metrics, calibration strategies, and validation guidelines. The framework will be applicable across domains (e.g., transport, economics, politics) and will inform future empirical studies and system designs.
metrics: consistency of persona traits over time, drift detection, alignment with human reference data, predictive accuracy

topic: diffusion model and generate videos
title: Novel Integration of Temporally-Aware GANs and Diffusion Models for Enhanced Video Generation

provenance.resources_text: {'datasets': 'We will use the Kinetics-400 dataset for training and validation.', 'compute': 'Access to GPUs with 16GB or more VRAM for model training.', 'software': 'TensorFlow or PyTorch for model development and evaluation.'}

motivation: The motivation behind this research is to address the limitations of current video generation models by integrating temporally-aware Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and diffusion models, which can lead to improved video quality and diversity. This integration aims to leverage the strengths of both GANs and diffusion models to produce more realistic and varied video outputs.
hypothesis: The integration of the temporally-aware GAN and the adapted DDPM will result in videos with higher quality and greater diversity compared to videos generated by either model alone, as measured by the Inception Score (IS) and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and additional temporal coherence metrics.
method_sketch: We will develop a novel tool library that integrates a temporally-aware GAN with a diffusion model for video generation. The GAN will be modified to incorporate temporal information, allowing it to better capture the dynamics of video sequences. The diffusion model will be a denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) adapted to handle temporal data. Our approach involves: 1) Modifying the StyleGAN2 architecture to include temporal convolutional layers that capture motion and sequence information. 2) Adapting the DDPM to process video frames sequentially, preserving temporal coherence. 3) Implementing a novel loss function that combines a cyclic loss with a temporal consistency loss to ensure both global structure and temporal coherence are maintained. The library will include modular components for easy adaptation and extension, allowing researchers to experiment with different GAN and diffusion model architectures.
expected_outcome: Our tool library will enable the generation of videos with higher visual quality, greater diversity, and improved temporal coherence. We aim to achieve an Inception Score (IS) of at least 250, Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) below 200, and a significant improvement in temporal coherence metrics for generated videos.
metrics: Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), temporal coherence metrics

topic: diffusion model and generate videos
title: OptiDiffuse+: A Novel Framework for Efficient Video Generation with Customized Diffusion Models

provenance.resources_text: {'datasets': ['MPI Kinetics', 'HMDB51'], 'compute': ['4x 16GB GPUs'], 'software': ['PyTorch', 'CUDA']}

motivation: Current diffusion models for video generation are computationally expensive and time-consuming to train, limiting their practical applications. OptiDiffuse+ aims to enhance video generation by proposing a novel framework that optimizes diffusion models specifically for video tasks, thereby democratizing access to high-quality video generation.
hypothesis: OptiDiffuse+ will reduce the training time of diffusion models for video generation by at least 30% and decrease VRAM usage by at least 20% while maintaining or improving the quality of generated videos compared to baseline models.
method_sketch: We will develop OptiDiffuse+, a novel framework that includes: 1. **Novel Temporal Adaptive Aggregation (TAA) Layers**: These layers will adaptively aggregate information across time steps based on a learned temporal importance map, preserving motion and temporal coherence while reducing computational load. 2. **Optimized Attention Mechanisms (OAM)**: We will design a new class of attention mechanisms tailored for video that leverages sparsity and redundancy patterns unique to video data. 3. **Dynamic Gradient Checkpointing (DGC)**: This approach will dynamically adjust the checkpointing strategy based on the model's current state to minimize memory overhead while maintaining performance. The framework will be benchmarked against existing tools on the MPI Kinetics dataset and an additional diverse video dataset, the HMDB51, to assess generalizability.
expected_outcome: OptiDiffuse+ will achieve a reduction in training time of at least 30%, maintain FID < 5 for generated videos, and reduce VRAM usage by at least 20% during training compared to baseline models on both the MPI Kinetics and HMDB51 datasets.

topic: Emergent Social Norms in LLM Agent Societies: How Unwritten Rules Develop Through Repeated Agent Interaction
title: Analyzing Emergent Social Norms and Bias in AI-Generated Agents through Evolutionary Psychology

provenance.resources_text: The study will require access to large-scale computational resources to run the multi-agent LLM simulations. Additionally, we will utilize existing datasets from evolutionary psychology studies, such as the 'Fundamental Social Motives' dataset, to benchmark and validate the AI-generated behaviors. Software-wise, we will leverage existing LLM frameworks like GPT and evolutionary algorithms libraries.

motivation: To bridge the gap between the study of emergent social norms in AI agent societies and evolutionary psychology, this study aims to explore how AI-generated social behaviors may reflect or contravene evolutionary-based social norms, providing a unique perspective on the ethical and societal implications of AI behavior.
hypothesis: AI-generated agent societies will exhibit behavior patterns that both reflect and diverge from evolutionary-based social norms, potentially introducing new biases and behaviors not observed in human societies.
method_sketch: This interdisciplinary study will employ a multi-pronged approach involving both empirical analysis and theoretical synthesis. First, we will develop a multi-agent LLM framework designed to simulate social interactions and the emergence of social norms, akin to those observed in human societies. Secondly, we will use evolutionary psychology frameworks to classify and analyze the emergent behaviors within this AI framework. The analysis will involve comparing AI-generated behaviors to known evolutionary social norms, identifying any biases, and assessing the potential societal impact of these behaviors.
expected_outcome: We anticipate identifying key similarities and differences between AI-generated social norms and those observed in human evolution. The measurable results will include a catalog of behaviors, a bias analysis report, and a framework for assessing the societal impact of AI-generated norms.

topic: Few-Shot Agent Adaptation via Meta-Prompting
title: Empirical and Theoretical Analysis of Neural Network Compatibility with Meta-Prompting in Few-Shot Learning

provenance.resources_text: We will utilize existing datasets like MiniImageNet and tieredImageNet for few-shot classification tasks. motivation: Few-shot learning is a critical challenge in AI, particularly when models must adapt with minimal data. Understanding this relationship can optimize meta-prompting strategies and improve few-shot performance across architectures. This research aims to provide a comprehensive analysis of the compatibility of various neural network architectures with meta-prompting in few-shot learning scenarios, combining both theoretical and empirical approaches.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that certain neural network architectures are more compatible with meta-prompting in few-shot scenarios due to inherent structural properties that facilitate faster adaptation and better generalization with minimal data. expected_outcome: We expect to derive specific conditions under which certain neural architectures outperform others in few-shot learning with meta-prompting. title: Bridging Equivariant Graph Neural Networks and Link Prediction: A Theoretical Framework for Geometric Link Inference

provenance.resources_text: No experimental datasets or compute needed for pure theoretical analysis. Required resources: access to literature, mathematical software (e.g., LaTeX, symbolic computation for examples), possibly proof-checking tools. The analysis may later be supported by simple synthetic experiments for illustration, but not required for the core work.

motivation: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have advanced both link prediction tasks (Cluster A) and scientific modeling with equivariant architectures (Cluster B). However, no existing work explores how equivariant GNNs can be leveraged for link prediction, especially in geometric domains where invariance/equivariance to symmetries is crucial. This gap presents an opportunity to develop a theoretical foundation that unifies these streams, enabling more robust and physically plausible link predictions in molecular, material, or protein interaction settings.
hypothesis: Equivariant GNNs can be systematically extended to perform link prediction while preserving symmetry properties, and these extensions can achieve universal approximation of any continuous invariant link function on geometric graphs, offering a principled advantage over non-equivariant methods in domains with inherent symmetries.
method_sketch: We will conduct a theoretical analysis to derive conditions under which equivariant GNNs can be adapted for link prediction, focusing on the interplay between symmetry constraints and link scoring functions. The analysis will: (1) formalize link prediction as a function on pairs of nodes in an equivariant graph; (2) characterize the constraints imposed by equivariance on the scoring function; (3) propose a general architectural blueprint that extends existing equivariant GNNs to produce link scores while preserving symmetries; (4) prove that such architectures are universal approximators of invariant link functions under certain conditions. The theoretical framework will be grounded in group representation theory and geometric deep learning principles.
expected_outcome: A set of theoretical results: (i) a formal definition of equivariant link prediction; (ii) necessary and sufficient conditions for an equivariant link scoring function; (iii) a constructive method to build such functions from existing equivariant GNN layers; (iv) a universality theorem. The outcome will be a peer-reviewed paper establishing the connection and providing a blueprint for future empirical work.

topic: hero Agents with Large Language Models
title: Full 2^4 Factorial Ablation of Modular LLM Agent Memory with Swap-Controlled Causal Retrieval Utility on Frozen-Replay Benchmarks

provenance.resources_text: GPT-4o API: ~$650 for 16 conditions × 812 WebArena tasks + 134 ALFWorld tasks at ~$0.05/task average; ~$120 for swap-counterfactual re-runs (1 additional call per retrieved chunk × estimated 3 chunks/step × 30 steps × 946 tasks × 16 conditions × 60% retrieval-active steps = ~820k calls at GPT-4o-mini pricing); WebArena Docker with --snapshot mode (open-source, 4× A100 GPUs via university HPC or Lambda Labs ~$200); ALFWorld Python simulator with serialized state checkpoints (open-source, CPU-only); FAISS 1.7.4 CPU; NetworkX 3.3 + spaCy en_core_web_trf; sentence-transformers 2.7 for swap index; statsmodels 0.14 for mixed-effects models; LangChain 0.2 agent scaffolding; 10 days researcher time for framework implementation and swap index construction.

motivation: Autonomous LLM agents on extended tasks require external memory, yet prior work bundles scratchpad summarization, episodic vector retrieval, semantic graph retrieval, and re-ranking into monolithic hybrid systems, making causal attribution of performance gains to specific components impossible. No study has applied a clean full-factorial design with all four components as orthogonal binary factors, nor measured retrieval utility causally while controlling for prompt-length and positional attention artifacts. This gap prevents principled memory architecture design for agentic systems.
hypothesis: H1: The scratchpad component S will show the largest independent main effect on task success rate (OR≥1.4, p<0.01 on WebArena) because it reduces context fragmentation regardless of retrieval strategy. H2: Episodic retrieval E will show higher mean swap-controlled causal utility on ALFWorld procedural tasks than semantic graph G (expected Δ≥0.10 on 0-1 scale), while G will show higher causal utility than E on WebArena knowledge-intensive subtasks (expected Δ≥0.08), as measured by the memory-type × task-type interaction in the causal utility model (p<0.05). H3: The re-ranking component C will show a significant positive main effect on WebArena task success (OR≥1.25, p<0.05) independent of which stores are active, demonstrating that recency-weighted re-ranking adds value beyond relevance-only retrieval even for single-store configurations.
method_sketch: Design a modular agent framework with four orthogonal binary memory flags: (S) 512-token scratchpad summarized every 10 steps by GPT-4o-mini; (E) FAISS-indexed episodic store of (step, observation, action) tuples retrieved by cosine similarity; (G) NetworkX entity-relation semantic graph built via spaCy en_core_web_trf NER+RE, queried by BFS from current task entities; (C) recency×relevance re-ranking applied to whatever stores are active (C=0 uses relevance-only ranking, making C independent of E and G). This yields a full 2^4=16 condition factorial. All conditions share GPT-4o backbone, identical system prompt, temperature=0, same tool APIs. Environment non-stationarity is eliminated by: (a) ALFWorld—using the Pydantic-serialized environment state checkpoints in the alfworld repo to freeze state at each decision point and replay all 16 conditions from identical checkpoints; (b) WebArena—using the Docker --headless --snapshot flag to produce bit-identical page states across conditions, validated by MD5-hashing rendered DOM snapshots for 50 tasks before full run. Causal retrieval utility is measured via a swap-in counterfactual design: for each retrieved chunk at each agent step, substitute it with a TF-IDF-matched but semantically orthogonal chunk drawn from a pre-built swap index over the task corpus (sentence-transformers all-MiniLM-L6-v2, maximum-marginal-relevance filter enforcing cosine similarity < 0.1 to original chunk, matched to within ±5 tokens of original chunk length). Delta-action-correctness between original and swapped conditions isolates content-specific utility from prompt-length and positional effects. Primary analysis: logistic mixed-effects model (lme4-style via Python statsmodels MixedLM) with four binary fixed effects S, E, G, C plus all six two-way interactions, task-id as random effect, outcome=task success (binary), estimated on WebArena (812 tasks) and ALFWorld (134 tasks) separately. Secondary: linear mixed-effects model on per-chunk causal utility scores with memory-type (episodic/semantic) × task-type (procedural/knowledge) as fixed effects. Power analysis: targeting 0.80 power at δ=0.08, α=0.05 for main-effect contrasts on WebArena (N=812) confirms adequate power; ALFWorld treated as exploratory with Bonferroni correction across 16 conditions.
expected_outcome: A 16×2 table of task success rates (WebArena, ALFWorld) with logistic mixed-effects model coefficients and 95% CIs for all four main effects and six two-way interactions. Expected: S main effect OR≈1.4 (p<0.01); C main effect OR≈1.25 (p<0.05) on WebArena; E×task-type and G×task-type interactions significant (p<0.05) in causal utility model. Swap-controlled causal utility distributions for episodic vs. semantic chunks across task types, publishable as the first prompt-structure-controlled measure of retrieval value in LLM agents. Full ablation table, causal utility analysis, and modular agent codebase released open-source.

topic: Improving the Performance of OFDM Systems in Dynamic Wireless channels Using Deep Learning Algorithms: Analysis and Evaluation of Hybrid Modulation Schemes
title: Chaos-GAN Augmented Bio-Inspired OFDM: A Hybrid Deep Learning Framework for Adaptive Covert Underwater Acoustic Communication

provenance.resources_text: Dolphin vocalization dataset (publicly available from NOAA, Macaulay Library), underwater acoustic channel simulator (e.g., Bellhop), deep learning framework (PyTorch/TensorFlow), GPU cluster (e.g., 4x RTX 3090 or cloud compute).

motivation: Current bionic CUAC systems (e.g., Hénon-based encryption with dolphin vocalizations) rely on static encryption and modulation, making them vulnerable to adaptive attacks and channel variations. A dynamic system that combines chaotic encryption with generative adversarial learning can adaptively optimize covertness and robustness in real time, addressing a critical gap in underwater communication security.
hypothesis: A chaos-based cGAN can generate dolphin-like OFDM symbols that are indistinguishable from real dolphin calls (e.g., >95% classification error for an eavesdropper) while maintaining low bit error rate (<10^-3) under realistic underwater acoustic channels (SNR 0–20 dB).
method_sketch: Design a hybrid framework integrating: (1) Modified chaotic map (e.g., 2D-Hénon-Sine) for key generation and symbol encryption; (2) A conditional GAN (cGAN) that generates dolphin-like waveforms from encrypted bits, with the discriminator trained to distinguish real dolphin sounds from synthetic ones, ensuring perceptual undetectability; (3) A lightweight CNN (e.g., MobileNetV2) at the receiver for robust signal detection under multipath and noise. The system operates as an autoencoder with chaotic encryption as a latent layer. Train on a dataset of real bottlenose dolphin vocalizations (e.g., from NOAA Ocean Noise Reference Station) and synthetic underwater channel simulations. Evaluate against passive eavesdropping (using a pretrained dolphin call classifier) and active jamming (adversarial noise injection).
expected_outcome: A proof-of-concept system demonstrating chaos-GAN augmented covert communication with measurable improvements in undetectability (eavesdropper's classification accuracy < 60%) and robustness (BER < 10^-3 at 10 dB SNR). Open-source code and trained models.

topic: LLM planning and reasoning with code generation
title: Formal Verification of Hardware Design via LLM-Assisted PDE Reasoning: A Theoretical Framework for Bridging Mathematical Correctness and RTL Synthesis

provenance.resources_text: No experimental resources required for theoretical analysis; we rely on literature review of both clusters, existing LLM models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, open-source math-specialized LLMs), and formal verification tools (e.g., model checkers). We may use symbolic computation libraries (SymPy) for illustrative examples.

motivation: Hardware design automation using LLM agents (Cluster B) faces challenges in ensuring correctness of generated RTL code, especially for safety-critical applications. Meanwhile, LLM-based methods for mathematical reasoning (Cluster A) have shown promise in autoformalization and solving PDEs, which require rigorous symbolic manipulation. By combining these, we can develop a theoretical framework where LLMs are used to reason about the mathematical properties of hardware designs (e.g., timing, concurrency, signal propagation) as partial differential equations or discrete dynamical systems, and then verify or refine the generated RTL code. This synergy leverages the strength of LLMs in mathematical reasoning to enhance the trustworthiness of LLM-generated hardware designs, while also providing a novel application domain for PDE/formal reasoning LLMs.
hypothesis: LLM agents equipped with PDE reasoning capabilities can produce formal proofs or high-confidence verification results for hardware designs generated by LLM-based code synthesis, reducing the need for extensive simulation and manual verification while maintaining or improving design quality.
method_sketch: We propose a theoretical analysis that models hardware behavior (e.g., digital circuits, signal flows) as systems of PDEs or difference equations capturing temporal and spatial dependencies. We then design a multi-agent LLM system where one agent translates RTL specifications into a formal mathematical representation (autoformalization), another agent performs reasoning and verification using PDE solving and symbolic methods, and a third agent refines the RTL code based on the analysis. The framework will be analyzed theoretically to determine conditions under which such a system can guarantee properties like functional correctness, timing closure, and conformance to specifications. We will also explore the integration of existing LLM-based PDE solvers (e.g., PDE-Controller) with hardware design agents (e.g., Spec2RTL-Agent) and define a common intermediate representation.
expected_outcome: A novel theoretical framework that defines the mapping between hardware design semantics and PDE-based models, a protocol for LLM agent collaboration, and a set of theorems or conditions for correctness. The outcome will be a published paper with conceptual diagrams and a roadmap for future empirical validation.

topic: LLM planning and reasoning with code generation
title: Unified Formalization-Execution Framework: Bridging LLM-Based Mathematical Reasoning and Code-Generating Agents for PDE Solving

provenance.resources_text: No physical resources required for theoretical analysis. Access to literature, possibly some symbolic computation software for prototyping ideas. For eventual empirical validation (outside scope of theoretical analysis) one would need PDE benchmark datasets (e.g., PDEBench, various canonical problems), LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, open-source models), and computational resources for running generated code, but this proposal is purely theoretical.

motivation: Current LLM-based approaches for solving PDEs (Cluster A) rely heavily on autoformalization and hybrid numerical methods, but they often produce symbolic or algorithmic outputs that are not directly executable or integrated with computational environments. Meanwhile, code-generating LLM agents (Cluster B) excel at producing runnable code for various tasks but lack deep mathematical reasoning and the ability to translate natural language descriptions of PDE problems into correct numerical implementations. By merging these two strengths, we can create a unified framework where an LLM first performs rigorous mathematical reasoning (e.g., autoformalization, problem decomposition, selection of appropriate numerical methods) and then generates executable, optimized code that directly solves the PDE, possibly with iterative refinement and verification. This synergy could significantly enhance the accessibility, accuracy, and efficiency of PDE solving, making it more usable for scientists and engineers without deep expertise in numerical methods.
hypothesis: A combined framework that integrates deep mathematical reasoning (autoformalization, method selection) with code generation and execution will produce more accurate and reliable solutions to PDE problems compared to either standalone LLM-based PDE solvers or generic code-generating agents, especially on complex, high-dimensional, or nonlinear PDEs where method selection and parameter tuning are critical.
method_sketch: We propose to develop a theoretical framework that defines a seamless pipeline from natural language description of a PDE problem (including boundary conditions, domain, and desired outputs) to a validated numerical solution. The framework will consist of: (1) a formal representation module that uses LLM-based autoformalization to convert the problem into a structured mathematical representation (e.g., using a formal language like Lean or as symbolic expressions); (2) a reasoning module that leverages LLM planning to decompose the problem, select appropriate numerical methods (finite element, finite difference, etc.), and generate a step-by-step plan; (3) a code generation module that translates the plan into executable code (Python, Julia, etc.) using verified libraries (e.g., FEniCS, Dedalus) and incorporates automatic verification and refinement loops (e.g., using LLM to analyze error metrics and adjust discretization). The theoretical analysis will focus on formalizing the interaction between these modules, proving correctness properties under certain assumptions, and analyzing the conditions under which the pipeline yields convergent solutions. We will also examine the information flow and error propagation, and propose metrics to evaluate the overall system.
expected_outcome: A comprehensive theoretical framework with formal definitions, theorems, and proofs showing that under certain conditions (e.g., LLM correctly formalizes the problem, method selection follows a known convergence guarantee, code generation is correct) the pipeline yields a solution within a bounded error. We will also produce a set of guidelines for implementing such a system and identify key challenges for future empirical work.
baselines: Cluster A, Cluster B, GPT-4, Claude, open-source models

motivation: The iterative self-improvement of Large Language Models (LLMs) is pivotal for advancing AI capabilities. Understanding how different algorithms, datasets, and metrics contribute to overcoming capability plateaus is crucial. This research aims to provide a structured comparative analysis that integrates survey meta-analysis with comparative analysis to identify effective strategies for enhancing LLM reasoning capabilities.
hypothesis: Different algorithms, datasets, and metrics will have varying effectiveness in overcoming capability plateaus in self-improving LLMs. The structured comparative analysis will identify the most effective strategies and provide insights into their underlying mechanisms.
method_sketch: We will conduct an interdisciplinary bridge approach that systematically reviews studies involving iterative self-improving LLMs. To integrate survey meta-analysis with comparative analysis, we will: 1) Establish a standardized taxonomy of methods used to measure capability gain and plateau across studies. 2) Develop rigorous criteria for study inclusion in the meta-analysis to minimize bias. 3) Use statistical software for data aggregation and analysis, ensuring comparability across studies. 4) Perform a comparative analysis of algorithms, datasets, and metrics, controlling for architectural differences, training procedures, and environmental interactions using multivariable analysis or sensitivity analyses. 5) Validate the comparability of results through a reconciliation process that harmonizes data from different studies.
expected_outcome: The expected outcome is a comprehensive review that provides a quantitative summary of the impact of different algorithms, datasets, and metrics on capability gain and plateaus in self-improving LLMs. The structured approach will help identify the most effective strategies for overcoming plateaus and gaining insights into their underlying mechanisms, informing future research directions and potentially leading to the development of more effective training strategies.

title: The Illusion of Alignment in Artificial Leviathans: A Theoretical Analysis of Norm Emergence in LLM-Based Multi-Agent Societies

provenance.resources_text: No large datasets or heavy compute required beyond typical academic resources. We will use standard scientific computing tools (Python, Mathematica) for analytical derivations and agent-based simulations. Access to literature and existing empirical results from both clusters is needed. Possibly small-scale pilot experiments with LLM agents to validate model assumptions, but not required for the theoretical analysis.

motivation: The recent surge in using LLM-based multi-agent systems to simulate social dynamics (Cluster B) has raised critical questions about the validity of such simulations, especially concerning the alignment of individual agents and the emergence of social norms. At the same time, Cluster A highlights that LLMs function as 'social catalysts' rather than moral agents, and that their alignment is an illusion when embedded in societies. Combining these perspectives is crucial: we need a rigorous theoretical framework to understand how the properties of LLMs as individual 'social catalysts' influence the macro-level emergence of norms and social contracts in artificial societies. This will help assess whether LLM-based simulations can reliably inform social science or are inherently biased by the training data and the nature of LLM social interaction. The synergy between the two clusters allows us to treat LLM agents not as human-like individuals but as artifacts that catalyze certain social patterns; we can then analyze the conditions under which these patterns reflect human-like norms or diverge due to LLM-specific artifacts. This is a substantial theoretical advance because it moves beyond empirical case studies to a principled understanding of the limits and possibilities of LLM-based social simulation.
hypothesis: We hypothesize that in LLM-based multi-agent societies, the emergence of social norms is heavily influenced by (a) the distribution of training data, which shapes the agents' priors on acceptable behavior, and (b) the 'social catalyst' effect, where small biases in initial interactions are amplified, leading to norms that may not reflect any individual agent's alignment goals. Furthermore, we predict that the resulting norms will be more sensitive to initial conditions and less robust to perturbation than norms emerging from human-like agents with intrinsic moral reasoning. The model will show that the alignment of individual LLM agents is neither necessary nor sufficient for the emergence of desirable social outcomes, thus supporting the 'illusion of alignment' thesis.
method_sketch: We will develop a formal theoretical model that abstracts key features of LLM agents (e.g., parametric knowledge, response stochasticity, tendency to conform to prompts) and their interactions in a multi-agent setting. The model will draw from game theory, evolutionary dynamics, and the theory of norm emergence. We will define a set of assumptions based on empirical observations from Cluster A (e.g., LLMs exhibit a 'social catalyst' effect, where they amplify existing biases and generate emergent conventions that are not aligned with individual intentions). We will then analyze the model to derive conditions under which stable social norms emerge, and compare these conditions to those known from human social dynamics (e.g., from classical social contract theories as in Cluster B's 'Artificial Leviathan'). The analysis will involve mathematical derivations, agent-based simulations of the abstract model (not full LLM simulations) to test theoretical predictions, and sensitivity analyses. The model will be parameterized to capture the degree of 'alignment' (or the illusion thereof) and the influence of training data distribution.
expected_outcome: The primary outcome is a theoretical framework that explains norm emergence in LLM-based artificial societies, highlighting the role of LLM-specific properties. This will yield a set of testable propositions, a classification of potential artifacts, and guidelines for designing more reliable social simulations. We expect to publish a high-impact paper that bridges AI and social science, potentially influencing future research directions in multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Quantifying the Emergence of Social Norms and Over-Competition in LLM-Based Artificial Societies: A Multi-Agent Simulation with Controlled Survival Pressure

provenance.resources_text: OpenAI/GPT-4 API or open-source LLMs (LLaMA 3) via Hugging Face; compute for running simulations (cloud instances with multiple GPUs if using local models); simulation framework (Mesa or custom Python); data storage for logs; analysis libraries (NetworkX, pandas, matplotlib).

motivation: Recent studies have explored norm emergence and collective behavior in LLM-based multi-agent systems, but they often treat LLMs as either social catalysts or as agents with survival instincts separately. Combining insights from both clusters allows us to systematically investigate how survival pressure (e.g., resource scarcity) influences the emergence of social norms and the tendency towards over-competition. This integrated approach addresses open questions from both clusters: how LLM agents develop norms under varying levels of resource constraints, whether survival instincts lead to biased or harmful collective behaviors, and what validity criteria are needed for such simulations. The synergy lies in using a Sugarscape-style environment (from cluster B) to create a controlled testbed for studying norm emergence and alignment (from cluster A), thereby enabling a more comprehensive understanding of the conditions under which LLM societies become cooperative or competitive.
hypothesis: Under high survival pressure, LLM agents will exhibit increased over-competition (higher inequality, more conflicts) and develop norms that favor individual survival over cooperation, regardless of alignment. However, aligned agents will show a delayed onset of over-competition and more cooperative norms even under scarcity, but these effects will diminish as pressure increases. Additionally, the emergence of norms will be more volatile and less stable in scenarios with mixed alignment populations.
method_sketch: We will design a multi-agent simulation where LLM agents (e.g., GPT-4, LLaMA) interact in a Sugarscape-inspired grid world with renewable resources. Agents must harvest resources to survive and can communicate to form norms (e.g., sharing, territoriality). We manipulate two independent variables: (1) the level of survival pressure (resource abundance vs. scarcity) and (2) the alignment of agents (pre-trained vs. instruction-tuned vs. explicitly prompted with ethical guidelines). Agents will be prompted to act autonomously, with their decisions generated via API calls. Each simulation run will record agent actions, communications, resource distribution, and survival rates. We will analyze the emergence of norms using network analysis of communication content and behavioral patterns. Over-competition will be measured via Gini coefficient of resources, frequency of conflicts, and extinction events. We will also implement the PIMMUR principles to validate collective behavior.
expected_outcome: Quantitative metrics on norm emergence (e.g., convergence to specific conventions) and competition levels across conditions. Identification of tipping points where cooperation breaks down. Insights into how alignment techniques affect collective behavior under stress. A set of validity guidelines for future LLM-based social simulations.
metrics: Gini coefficient of resources, frequency of conflicts, extinction events

title: Design and Validation of a Multi-Modality Phantom for Standardized Radiomics Feature Extraction in PET/CT and PET/MRI

provenance.resources_text: 3D printing facilities, materials for tissue mimicry (e.g., epoxy resins, gadolinium-doped gels, radioactive isotopes), access to PET/CT and PET/MRI scanners, radiomics software (e.g., PyRadiomics), statistical analysis tools.

motivation: Radiomics features extracted from PET, CT, and MRI images are increasingly used for tumor characterization, but their reproducibility across different imaging platforms and protocols is limited. Existing phantoms often focus on a single modality or lack the complexity needed to mimic heterogeneous tumor textures and activity distributions. A standardized, multi-modality phantom that incorporates realistic heterogeneity and can be used across PET/CT and PET/MRI systems would enable calibration and validation of radiomics features, facilitating multicenter studies and clinical translation.
hypothesis: A well-designed multi-modality phantom with controlled heterogeneity can serve as a standard for evaluating and harmonizing radiomics features across different imaging platforms, leading to improved reproducibility and reliability of radiomics in clinical studies.
method_sketch: Design a modular 3D-printed phantom with compartments that can be filled with materials mimicking different tissue densities (CT), relaxation times (MRI), and radiotracer uptake (PET). Activity painting techniques will be used to create non-uniform distributions of radioactivity within compartments, simulating tumor heterogeneity. The phantom will include features of varying size, shape, and texture to challenge radiomics algorithms. A set of reference radiomics features will be derived from high-resolution ground-truth scans (micro-CT, high-field MRI) of the phantom. The phantom will be scanned on multiple PET/CT and PET/MRI systems with varying acquisition parameters to assess feature reproducibility. Statistical analysis (ICC, coefficient of variation) will quantify the impact of scanner type and settings on feature stability.
expected_outcome: A prototype phantom with documented design and manufacturing process; a dataset of phantom scans across multiple scanners; a set of radiomics features that are robust across platforms; guidelines for using the phantom for quality assurance and feature harmonization.

title: A Formal Framework for Ethical Constrained Generation in Foundation Models via Causal Abstraction

provenance.resources_text: Theoretical work requiring no computational resources beyond a laptop; collaboration with ethicists for constraint specification. For validation, we may simulate on small transformer models.

motivation: Foundation models generate content that can cause harm (bias, misinformation, toxicity). Current methods for ethical alignment (RLHF, constitutional AI) are empirical and lack formal guarantees. We propose a theoretical framework that enforces ethical constraints by imposing causal abstractions on the generative process, allowing provable bounds on the probability of generating harmful outputs.
hypothesis: Ethical constraints can be formally embedded into foundation models via causal abstraction, leading to provable safety guarantees without sacrificing generative diversity.
method_sketch: We will develop a mathematical model of generative AI as a stochastic process over a causal graph representing concepts. Using category theory, we define a notion of ethical constraints as morphisms that restrict the space of allowable outputs. We then prove that by applying a causal abstraction (a quotient of the causal graph) we can guarantee that any generated output respects the constraints up to a specified epsilon. The framework will be instantiated on toy models and then extended to large language models by analyzing their internal representations as causal variables.
expected_outcome: A set of theorems and algorithms for ethical constraint enforcement, with a proof-of-concept implementation on a small language model demonstrating reduced harmful outputs while preserving perplexity.

title: SemStigma: Semantic Communication-Augmented Stigmergic Coordination for UAV Swarm Wildfire Response

provenance.resources_text: AirSim simulator with custom wildfire plugin (open-source, available on GitHub); FARSITE fire spread model for generating realistic fire dynamics; PyTorch 2.x for semantic encoder and DDQN training; 4× NVIDIA A100 GPUs for parallel AirSim environment rollouts; ROS2 Humble for swarm middleware simulation; no proprietary datasets required — all environments are procedurally generated using FARSITE parameters calibrated to California chaparral fuel models (publicly documented).

motivation: Existing UAV swarm wildfire systems (Cluster A) rely on raw sensor data broadcast and explicit message passing, creating bandwidth bottlenecks as swarm size scales. Semantic communication frameworks from V2X research (Cluster B) compress transmissions to task-relevant meaning, but have never been applied to stigmergic coordination signals in drone swarms. Bridging these clusters enables swarms to operate in bandwidth-constrained, smoke-degraded RF environments typical of active wildfires.
hypothesis: A swarm of 20 UAVs using semantic-token stigmergy will achieve ≥90% fire perimeter coverage within 8 minutes on a 1 km² simulated wildfire scenario while consuming ≤40% of the inter-UAV communication bandwidth required by a baseline ACO+raw-broadcast system, with suppression agent utilization efficiency improving by ≥15% over the non-semantic ACO baseline from the 2023 nature-inspired swarming paper.
method_sketch: Design a three-layer architecture: (1) Semantic Encoder Layer — each UAV runs a lightweight transformer-based semantic encoder (DistilBERT-style, <5M params) trained to compress pheromone-analog state vectors (fire intensity, coverage density, battery level, suppression agent remaining) into 32-bit semantic tokens rather than raw 256-bit state broadcasts; (2) Stigmergic Field Layer — a distributed virtual pheromone grid maintained via gossip protocol where each node updates only semantic-token deltas, using ACO-style evaporation rate τ=0.05/s and reinforcement factor η proportional to IR sensor confidence; (3) Reactive Control Layer — a DDQN policy (state: local semantic token + 8-neighbor pheromone gradient, action: 6-DOF movement + suppression trigger) pre-trained in AirSim wildfire simulation then fine-tuned with federated learning across swarm nodes. System diagram: UAV_i → [IR Sensor + LiDAR] → Semantic Encoder → 32-bit token → Gossip Broadcast → Shared Pheromone Grid → DDQN Policy → Motor Commands + Suppression Actuator. Ground station receives compressed semantic summaries only, not raw telemetry.
expected_outcome: Quantitative: ≥90% fire perimeter coverage vs ~75% for ACO-only baseline; ≤40% bandwidth usage vs raw-broadcast baseline measured in bits/second/UAV; ≥15% improvement in suppression agent efficiency (kg agent per m² fire suppressed); swarm coordination latency <200ms per decision cycle at 20-node scale. Qualitative: a reusable open-source ROS2 package implementing SemStigma middleware, and a benchmark suite of 10 procedurally generated wildfire scenarios for future comparison.
metrics: fire perimeter coverage, suppression agent efficiency, swarm coordination latency, bandwidth usage
